quoteLast: , be at the Carmelite Church, alone, and go to the first confessional on the left as you enter. You will find the door open."
title: The War of Women, Volume 2
author: Dumas, Alexandre, 1802-1870

quoteFirst: Colin arrived at his mother's house
quoteTime: about six o'clock in the evening
quoteLast: , and, by previous appointment, met there with his friend Fanny. Together they put everything into a state of preparation; while Colin, as a precautionary measure, in case anything unfortunate should happen, obliged the young woman to take three guineas of the fifteen which Mr.
title: Colin Clink, Volume 2 (of 3)
author: Hooton, Charles, 1813?-1847; Cruikshank, George, 1792-1878 [Illustrator]; Leech, John, 1817-1864 [Illustrator]

quoteTime: about six o’clock in the evening
quoteLast: , when the prisoners were all quiet in their respective yards, it being about the usual time of turning in for the night, and the greater part of the prisoners being then in the prisons, the alarm bell was rung, and many of the prisoners ran up to the Market-square to learn the occasion of the alarm.
title: The prisoners' memoirs, or, Dartmoor prison
author: Andrews, C. (Charles)

quoteFirst: "Mr. Ewart accompanied me to Hyde Park,
quoteLast: , where we met you and Mr. Mathews, and we walked together to the ring.--Mr. Mathews refusing to make any other acknowledgment than he had done, I observed that we were come to the ground: Mr. Mathews objected to the spot, and appealed to you.--We proceeded to the back of a building on the other side of the ring, the ground was there perfectly level.
title: Memoirs of the Life of the Rt. Hon. Richard Brinsley Sheridan — Volume 01
author: Moore, Thomas, 1779-1852

quoteFirst: I struck my head against a branch of one of these, and found that I had disturbed a large family which had taken up its residence upon it; my shoulders were quickly covered with small red ants, and I did not get rid of them without feeling some of their bites. We arrived at the dry lake
quoteTime: about six o’clock in the evening
quoteLast: , and put up at one of the cottages. In the course of the following morning, I made known my principal errand, and that I likewise wished to purchase another horse.
title: Travels in Brazil
author: Koster, Henry, 1793-1820?

quoteFirst: "For some days after this I was left in peace, and began to hope that my letter had appeased the spirit of the murdered man; but on the fifth day,
quoteTime: about six o'clock in the evening
quoteLast: , happening to want some papers that I had left in the pocket of a coat which was hanging in the closet, I went in to get them. I took in no candle, as it was not yet dark, but left the door wide open to light me.
title:
John Thorndyke's Cases
related by Christopher Jervis and edited by R. Austin Freeman
author: Freeman, R. Austin (Richard Austin), 1862-1943; Brock, H. M. (Henry Matthew), 1875-1960 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: The _Cornwallis_ was lying on our port side a few hundred yards away, so that about eleven I went on board, and, with Armitage, started off to look for something to shoot, among the hummocks, three or four miles north of where we lay. We spent hours tramping over the ice, but did not see a track, so we returned to our ships
quoteLast: This hummock belt extended east and west and had been caused by the rafting of great floes. It was quite smooth from the ship to the hummocks and also on the other side of them.
title: A Voyage to the Arctic in the Whaler Aurora
author: Lindsay, David Moore, 1862-

quoteFirst: A small pine-tree was picked up much shattered by the ice. In the afternoon of the 30th a very dense fog came on; and
quoteLast: when sailing before a fresh breeze we were suddenly involved in a heavy stream of ice. Considerable difficulty was experienced in steering through the narrow channels between the different masses in this foggy weather, and the ship received several severe blows.
title: The Journey to the Polar Sea
author: Franklin, John, 1786-1847; Scott, Robert Falcon, 1868-1912 [Author of introduction, etc.]

quoteFirst: I myself was carried thither, having received five wounds in different parts of the body, my left hand shattered, and two nasty wounds in the head. I was totally unconscious when taken out of the Redan, and for some hours afterwards. At
quoteLast: , I found myself in our front trench, with a dead 33rd man lying across me; I got him off the best way I could, and then tried to get up, but found that I could not stand, for I had almost bled to death. Dr. Hale, V.C., did all he could for me; I then had to remain and take my chance or turn of being carried to camp, where I arrived about 7·30 p.m., when my wounds were dressed, and a good cup of beef tea revived me; there I had to remain for upwards of three months, but, with careful attendance, and a good, strong constitution, I was, by that time, ready for them
title:
A Soldier's Experience; or, A Voice from the Ranks
Showing the Cost of War in Blood and Treasure. A Personal Narrative of the Crimean Campaign, from the Standpoint of the Ranks; the Indian Mutiny, and Some of its Atrocities; the Afghan Campaigns of 1863
author: Gowing, T. (Timothy), 1834-1908

quoteTime: about six o'clock in the evening
quoteLast: , and Chick had just returned from having a confidential talk with one of the stage hands of the theater in which the then famous attraction, the mammoth European and American vaudeville troupe, of which Señora Cervera was a star attraction, had for several months been playing to crowded houses.
title: With Links of Steel; Or, The Peril of the Unknown
author: Carter, Nicholas (House name)
